clinical_coherence.notes: 41F right-handed Hispanic with new-onset seizures and comorbid anxiety and migraine. The clinical presentation features an atypical aura (epigastric rising with intense fear and depersonalization) that overlaps with panic disorder, creating genuine diagnostic complexity. Remote febrile seizure at age 2 provides appropriate risk factor. The neurological exam shows subtle left temporal dysfunction (mild verbal memory impairment on bedside testing). Realistic medication list and social history.

tool_output_consistency.notes: EEG shows left temporal sharp waves but infrequent (2-3 per hour). MRI shows left hippocampal sclerosis that is subtle (mild volume loss, borderline T2 signal). Video-EEG captures seizures but semiology partially overlaps with panic attacks. The moderate MRI confidence (0.72) is appropriate given the subtle findings. One minor issue: the initial EEG description could better explain why the infrequent discharges make this more challenging.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD G40.109 correct. Differential appropriately includes panic disorder as a major competing diagnosis. Critical actions include both epilepsy workup and psychiatric evaluation. Key reasoning about panic disorder vs temporal lobe seizures is clinically important and accurate.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Genuinely moderate: subtle hippocampal sclerosis, infrequent interictal discharges, aura overlapping with panic disorder, comorbid anxiety disorder that could serve as an alternative explanation. The diagnostic uncertainty is well-calibrated.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 41F Hispanic with anxiety, migraine, and remote febrile seizure is realistic. Late onset of TLE symptoms (age 41) despite childhood febrile seizure is plausible and adds clinical interest.

educational_value.notes: Excellent teaching case for the panic disorder vs temporal lobe epilepsy differential, which is a common clinical challenge. The overlap in aura features (fear, epigastric sensation, depersonalization) is a high-yield teaching point. The subtle MRI findings teach attention to detail in imaging interpretation.

strengths: Panic disorder vs TLE differential is a genuinely challenging and common clinical scenario
Subtle hippocampal sclerosis requires careful MRI interpretation
Aura semiology overlapping with panic attacks is realistic and educational
Well-calibrated moderate difficulty with appropriate diagnostic uncertainty
